most people hit their Claude limit way faster than they need to, and it’s almost always fixable with a few small tweaks 😅

here are the three I run every day:

🎯 default to Sonnet. save Opus for real strategy and big brain thinking. Opus eats your weekly limit about 5x faster.

✂️ when a chat gets long, ask Claude to summarise it into bullets and paste those into a fresh chat. in Claude Code just type /compact.

🪟 new topic = new chat. always. long chats make Claude re-read the entire history every single message, and that’s where your tokens go.

🦴 bonus for Claude Code users: drop “no filler, three to six word sentences, direct answers only” into your CLAUDE.md file. output drops about 40%.

If you want a full guide, cheat sheet, a model-pick table, and a printable checklist - comment TOKENS and I’ll DM it to you 💛💌

these 5 prompts changed how I use AI every single day (btw these apply to ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini or any LLM) 🧠

here are my top 5 cheat codes:

predict → uses your chat history to predict outcomes

kill critic → stops it nodding along to everything you say
explain like I’m 10 → strips out the jargon

10x think → forces it to slow down and reason properly

human → makes the output sound like a real person, not AI

Here are 4 free open-source tools to slash Claude costs by up to 90%👀

🪨 caveman: forces Claude to reply in ultra-compressed “caveman speak.” Same answer, 70% fewer words, way less token spend (But!! If you’re trying to understand how things work, it’s going to be really difficult to understand because remember it’s optimising for speed not comprehension lol). (Get it here: github.com/JuliusBrussee/caveman)

📂 lean-ctx: remembers every file Claude reads so the second read costs almost nothing. Cached re-reads drop from thousands of tokens to about 13. (github.com/yvgude/lean-ctx)

🔍 symdex: instead of Claude reading an entire file to find one function, symdex points it directly to the exact line it needs (works best for large code bases where the overhead for indexing is worth it) (github.com/husnainpk/SymDex)

🧩 context-skills: loads only the instructions Claude needs for the current task, instead of feeding it everything at once. Keeps the context window lean from the start.

Stack all four = roughly 10x less context burned per session. But quick note, all of these exist because Anthropic’s pricing model incentivises token compression. The moment Anthropic ships better native caching or cheaper pricing tiers, most of these tools become redundant overnight…

I was making Meta money but still unhappy even though I was able to work remotely in over 10 different countries while working in Asia.

I realized that I didn’t want financial freedom at a certain point because it meant giving up a lot of the experiences that I could have earlier in my twenties.

Time freedom was something that you really can’t get back. The ability to make money on your own terms without having to constantly feel an overwhelming pressure and stress.

Everyone told me I was crazy for walking away from $500k. “You’ll regret it.” “Just grind for a few more years.” “You have the dream job.”

But here’s the truth: a dream job that leaves you drained isn’t a dream. It’s a trap dressed up in a nice salary.

Your twenties are for building, exploring, failing, and figuring out who you actually are, not just optimizing for a number in your bank account.

Bet on yourself while you still have the energy to.

Is AI saturated? This is why you need to start getting into AI #explore #ai #tech

The hardest part isn’t the money. It’s losing the status.

It took me a while to realize how much of my confidence was rented.

When I said “I work at Meta,” people leaned in. They assumed I was smart. They wanted to hear more. Instant credibility without saying anything else.

Now I say I’m working on a startup or doing content creation and I watch their face change in real time.

“Oh… cool. So like an AI wrapper?” or “yeah typical”

But its always been the same me. Same skills I had two weeks ago. But suddenly I have to prove myself again. The benefit of the doubt is gone.

I didn’t realize how much I was leaning on that logo. How many doors it opened before I even knocked. How many conversations it carried when I had nothing interesting to say.

Turns out a lot of the respect I thought I earned was just borrowed from a brand.

The weird part? I’m the same engineer who shipped features to millions of users. The same person who passed those interviews, solved those problems, got those promotions. But without the logo, I’m just another guy with an idea and no traction.

My LinkedIn still says “Ex-Meta SWE” and I catch myself leading with it. Using my past to validate my present. Still renting credibility from a place I don’t work anymore.

The hardest part of betting on yourself is realizing how much of your confidence was never really yours.

Learn AI with me day 2: Gemini 3 #tech

5 things I learned about AI from going to NeurIPS, the biggest AI conference

1. Everyone’s going for human data and reinforcement learning, this is essentially how AI models can get smarter and provide better answers for every situation because they need more data to train on. Essentially If you’re not working in an AI lab, you’re probably working to provide data to labs.

2. There were a lot of respect in the room among frontier researchers for Nano banana pro, anthropic opus 4.5 and xAI

3. Benchmarks dont mean anything in production

4. AI is not going to hit AGI with the current models we are building but that doesn’t really matter

5. The biggest theme being continual learning, how do we build AI that actually improves over time without forgetting everything it knew before, one talk that stuck with me was developing LLMs that can reason by arguing with themselves

Quitting Meta was supposed to be freedom. Instead, I spent the first month trapped in a spreadsheet, calculating if I could afford coffee.

Here’s what nobody tells you about leaving Big Tech: the engineer mindset that made you successful there will sabotage you on the outside. I was optimizing for the wrong metric entirely.

The wake-up call came when I realized I’d spent 3 weeks trying to stretch my runway by 60 days instead of spending 3 days figuring out how to generate my first dollar.

This video is about the psychological trap most people fall into after quitting and why the skills that got you promoted at FAANG are exactly what will keep you broke as a founder.
I’m documenting this journey in real-time. The mistakes, the pivots, the money running down. No highlight reel, just what actually happens when you trade the golden handcuffs for uncertainty.
